talk.title: The Quiet Architecture of Slow Software
talk.speaker: Mara Vint (fictional)
beats: Hook — we measure machine speed (p99 latency) but never the speed at which a tool gets out of your way.
Claim — 'slow software' is software designed at the pace of understanding; slowness is a design budget, not a defect.
Three forces push the other way: the demo-driven roadmap, the metric monoculture, the novelty treadmill.
Worked contrast — a 'fast' tool with 40 unnameable features vs a 'slow' tool with 9 that compose; same job, fewer moves.
The turn — this is subtraction under constraint, and it is fundable if you can price the surface you didn't cut.
The ask — budget for refinement, not just features; measure time-to-out-of-the-way.

audience: Design-literate engineers and EMs at a craft conference; presented live from a laptop in a dark room.
avoid: real benchmarks or made-up precise metrics presented as real

images.policy: placeholder blocks; image prompts only; do NOT generate real images

theme_hint: editorial restraint — warm paper + a single ink accent
